{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

In a **randomized controlled trial** (RCT),
treatment is assigned by the investigator using a random mechanism,
independent of any characteristics of the participants.
This makes the treatment groups **exchangeable**:
the distribution of potential outcomes is the same
in treated and untreated groups.

As a result, in a perfectly randomized trial:

$$\E{Y(1)} = \E{Y \mid A = 1}
\quad \text{and} \quad
\E{Y(0)} = \E{Y \mid A = 0}$$

The observed mean difference between treatment groups
is an unbiased estimate of the ATE:

$$\widehat{\text{ATE}} = \bar{Y}_{A=1} - \bar{Y}_{A=0}$$

::: notes

Randomization achieves unconditional exchangeability:
$A \perp\!\!\!\perp (Y(0), Y(1))$.
Because there are no common causes of treatment assignment
and the outcome,
there is no confounding.
The observed association between $A$ and $Y$ in the data
is a direct measure of the causal effect.

:::

## The HERS study: a randomized trial example {#sec-causal-hers-rct}

<!-- Include paths below are relative to the project root (standard for Quarto) -->
{{< include _subfiles/shared/_sec_hers_intro.qmd >}}

::: {#fig-hers-swimmer}

```{r}
#| code-fold: true

hers <- readRDS(fs::path_package("rme", "extdata/hers.rds"))
# swimplot expects base data.frame column indexing.
hers <- as.data.frame(hers)
n_per_arm_display <- 20
follow_up_end <- 1

# Keep only participants with both baseline and year-1 LDL values.
hers_swimmer <- hers[!is.na(hers$LDL) & !is.na(hers$LDL1), ]
# Sort by treatment arm,
# then by LDL change,
# for visual grouping.
hers_swimmer <- hers_swimmer[
  order(hers_swimmer$HT, hers_swimmer$LDL1 - hers_swimmer$LDL),
]
hers_swimmer <- do.call(
  rbind,
  lapply(
    split(hers_swimmer, hers_swimmer$HT),
    function(df_arm) {
      n_take <- min(n_per_arm_display, nrow(df_arm))
      df_arm[seq_len(n_take), ]
    }
  )
)

hers_swimmer$display_id <- paste("Participant", seq_len(nrow(hers_swimmer)))
hers_swimmer$start <- 0.1
hers_swimmer$end <- follow_up_end
hers_swimmer$arm <- ifelse(hers_swimmer$HT == 1, "Hormone therapy", "Placebo")
hers_swimmer$ldl_increased_at_year1 <- hers_swimmer$LDL1 > hers_swimmer$LDL

hers_events <- hers_swimmer[hers_swimmer$ldl_increased_at_year1, ]
if (nrow(hers_events) > 0) {
  hers_events$time <- follow_up_end
  hers_events$event <- "LDL increased at year 1"
}

hers_plot <- swimplot::swimmer_plot(
  df = hers_swimmer,
  id = "display_id",
  start = "start",
  end = "end",
  name_fill = "arm",
  identifiers = FALSE,
  width = 0.8
) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Follow-up time (years from baseline lipid measurement)",
    y = "Participant",
    fill = "Study arm",
    shape = "Observed year-1 outcome"
  ) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(
    ~HT,
    ncol = 1,
    scales = "free_y",
    labeller = ggplot2::labeller(
      HT = c("0" = "Placebo", "1" = "Hormone therapy")
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

if (nrow(hers_events) > 0) {
  hers_plot <- hers_plot +
    swimplot::swimmer_points(
      df_points = hers_events,
      id = "display_id",
      time = "time",
      name_shape = "event",
      size = 2.8
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_shape_manual(values = c("LDL increased at year 1" = 17))
}

hers_plot
```

Swimmer chart
using observed HERS participant data.
Bars show baseline-to-year-1 follow-up
for participants with both LDL and LDL1 measured;
triangles indicate participants
whose LDL increased at year 1.
For readability,
the figure displays up to 20 participants
per study arm,
with one facet for each arm.

:::

In HERS, hormone therapy (HT) was randomized,
so the treatment groups were exchangeable at baseline.
The observed difference in CHD event rates between
the HT and placebo groups estimates the causal effect of HT.

{{< include _subfiles/shared/_sec_hers_conclusions.qmd >}}

::: notes

The HERS result illustrates the gap between
observational evidence and causal evidence:
prior observational studies had suggested large benefits of HT,
but these reflected confounding by
the "healthy user" effect —
women who voluntarily used HT tended to be healthier overall.
Randomization in HERS removed this confounding.

:::

## Covariate adjustment in RCTs {#sec-causal-rct-adjustment}

Although randomization ensures no confounding,
adjusting for baseline covariates in an RCT can still be useful:

- **Improved precision**: Adjusting for strong predictors of the outcome
  reduces residual variance and narrows confidence intervals.
- **Covariate imbalance**: When randomization produces chance imbalances
  in small trials,
  adjustment can correct for these.

However, in an RCT,
covariates that are **mediators** of the treatment effect
(i.e., variables affected by treatment)
should generally **not** be adjusted for
when estimating the **total** causal effect of treatment.
Adjusting for mediators blocks part of the causal pathway
and gives only the **direct** effect of treatment.
